title: Wrap-Up — Is the U.S. Right for You?
description: Self-assessment reflection, resource downloads with digital credential, and clear next steps: 1:1 booking with UCF BIP team and Phase 2 overview.

lessons.title: Self-Assessment — Are You Ready?
objectives: Reflect on their U.S. market readiness across all seven module topics
Identify specific areas where they need more support or professional guidance
Assess whether U.S. market expansion is realistic for their company and business stage

contentBlocks.title: Arriving at UCF Headquarters: What You Now Know
contentBlocks.content:
After seven modules and dozens of topics, you have arrived at the final destination on this road trip: the UCF campus and its Business Incubation Program headquarters. Picture the modern buildings, the research labs, the incubator offices where startups are already working. This is home base. But how much do you actually remember, and more importantly, where are the gaps you still need to fill?

Standing here on campus, it is time to take an honest look at where you stand. You covered seven modules. Having info is not the same as being ready. The goal here is to know where the gaps are and what help you need before you spend money.

The Four Themes as Your Decision Filter

Here's how this plays out: before you sign anything, hire anyone, or transfer money, recall the four themes:

  • Reduce Risk . Avoid the legal, financial, and operational traps.
  • Watch Your Money . Know the realistic costs before committing.
  • Avoid Common Mistakes . Don't repeat the errors other founders make.
  • Understand the Culture . Know how American business actually works.

contentBlocks.title: Did You Know?
contentBlocks.content: UCF enrolls over 70,000 students, making it one of the largest universities in the United States by enrollment. The UCF Business Incubation Program has helped launch and grow hundreds of companies since 1999, generating billions in revenue across Central Florida. The university's research park, located adjacent to campus, is home to more than 100 high-tech companies and research labs, creating a dense innovation corridor that founders can tap into from day one.

contentBlocks.title: Important Reminder: This Was Educational Guidance
contentBlocks.content: Now, consider this carefully. Everything you covered in this program was general educational information intended to help you understand the U.S. business environment. It is not legal, tax, immigration, or financial advice. Before you take action on any topic: entity formation, banking, taxes, IP, hiring, or visas, consult a qualified professional who can review your specific situation. Knowledge is the starting point. Professional guidance is how you turn knowledge into safe action.

contentBlocks.title: Your Readiness Self-Assessment
contentBlocks.content:
Before you leave headquarters and head into the U.S. market, you need a readiness review. This is yours. For each topic, honestly rate yourself: clear on what to do, or still have major questions? No passing score. The point is clarity.

Module 1: Legal Setup

Is your foundation solid? Entity, EIN, registered agent — these are the structural systems that keep everything else from collapsing.

  • Do you know which entity type fits your business goals and visa situation?
  • Have you checked [SunBiz](https://sunbiz.org) for name availability?
  • Do you understand the registered agent requirement?
  • Do you know what documents you need for a U.S. business bank account?

If unclear, consult a business attorney or CPA before filing.

Module 2: Finances and Taxes

Are your accounts and funding in order? Banking, taxes, accounting — these power everything that follows once you step out of headquarters.

  • Can you explain federal income tax vs. state sales tax?
  • Do you understand how U.S. sales tax differs from VAT?
  • Have you identified an accountant who understands international founders?

If unclear, an international-focused accountant saves you from penalties that cost far more than their fee.

Module 3: Protecting Your Business

Are your legal protections in place? IP, insurance, and contracts are your safety net — invisible until you need them, catastrophic without them.

  • Does your home-country IP protection cover you in the U.S.?
  • Can you list the insurance types your business needs?
  • Have you identified which contracts you need before starting?

If uncertain, an IP attorney conversation is a high-priority next step.

Module 4: Building Your Team

Is your team assembled? Classification, payroll, compliance — your support team is critical, and getting the roster wrong stalls you before you start.

  • Do you understand contractor vs. employee in the U.S.?
  • Are you aware of true hiring cost (salary plus 15-30% overhead)?
  • Do you know your visa authorization status?

Misclassifying a worker can result in back taxes, penalties, and legal liability.

contentBlocks.title: Readiness Check — Markets, Culture, and Ecosystem
contentBlocks.content:
Now, let's continue with the remaining modules.

Module 5: Understanding U.S. Customers

Is your market strategy configured for the U.S.? Product adaptation, pricing, acquisition strategy — these determine whether your business actually reaches your target market.

  • Have you researched your U.S. competitive set?
  • Do you know how U.S. pricing expectations differ from your home market?
  • Do you understand vendor onboarding requirements (W-9, insurance certificates, procurement)?
  • Can you describe your first U.S. customer acquisition strategy?

If you haven't done U.S.-specific competitive research, block time for that before selling.

Module 6: U.S. Business Culture

Have you studied the local conditions? Communication, meetings, trust-building — this is the atmosphere you will be operating in, and misjudging it stalls even the best-prepared founders.

  • Can you explain American friendliness vs. business commitment?
  • Do you know follow-up expectations after meetings?
  • Are you aware of how directness and decision-making differ in U.S. business?

Cultural missteps cost real money. If these feel abstract, find a mentor who has operated in both cultures.

Module 7: Central Florida Ecosystem

Do you know your advisory network? SBDC, chambers, [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) — no founder succeeds without a support team on the ground.

  • Can you name three organizations offering free support to international founders?
  • Do you know Central Florida's advantages vs. Miami, NYC, or Silicon Valley?
  • Are you aware of what phases 2 and 3 of the [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) program offer?

If you cannot name specific resources you plan to contact, revisit Module 7.

contentBlocks.title: Founder meeting with a mentor to review business readiness before launch
Where Do You Need More Help?
contentBlocks.content:
No preparation is perfect. The smartest founders identify what is missing and get help before they leave headquarters. Feeling swamped is normal. You are not supposed to be an expert in all seven areas. That is exactly why UCF's campus has an entire ecosystem of advisors, mentors, and professional connections waiting in the buildings around you. You need to know enough to ask the right questions and avoid expensive mistakes.

Where to Get Help

  • Legal (entity, contracts, compliance) . Business attorney with international experience. SBDC connects you with pro bono legal clinics.
  • Taxes and accounting . CPA with international experience. Ask: "Do you work with foreign-owned businesses?"
  • Immigration and visas . Immigration attorney. Not optional.
  • IP protection . IP attorney. [USPTO](https://www.uspto.gov) has resources, but filing strategy needs professional guidance.
  • Banking . Start with the bank directly. Bring documents; expect the first visit to be a consultation.
  • Customer acquisition . SBDC and SCORE offer free advising. [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) connects you with mentors.
  • General guidance . The [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) team is available for a conversation once you complete Module 8.

Your readiness scores are your map. Prioritize professionals based on your lowest scores.

tabs.label: Download Resources
tabs.content:
**Throughout this course, we've referenced key documents and tools.** Here's your consolidated list:

- [SunBiz](https://sunbiz.org) entity registration: sunbiz.org
- [IRS EIN](https://www.irs.gov/businesses/small-businesses-self-employed/employer-id-numbers) application: irs.gov/businesses
- [Florida SBDC](https://floridasbdc.org) consultation: floridasbdc.org
- SCORE mentorship: score.org/orlando
- [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) programs: ucf.edu/bip

**Save these links** — you'll reference them as you build your U.S. presence.

tabs.label: Phase 2 Preview
tabs.content:
**Phase 2 of the [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) program** takes you from knowledge to action.

**What to expect:**
- Hands-on mentorship with Central Florida business leaders
- Market validation workshops
- Pitch development and investor connections
- Legal and accounting office hours

**Duration:** 8–12 weeks

**Eligibility:** Completion of this Phase 1 course + application review

tabs.label: Get Support
tabs.content:
**You don't have to do this alone.** Here are your immediate next steps:

1. **Schedule a free SBDC consultation** — get personalized guidance on your specific situation
2. **Apply to [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) Phase 2** — take your business to the next level
3. **Join the UCF entrepreneur community** — connect with fellow international founders

**Remember:** Every successful international founder in the U.S. started exactly where you are right now.

contentBlocks.title: One of the Biggest Risks Is Going It Alone
contentBlocks.content: Think about this: you now know more than many international founders who enter the U.S. market without structured preparation. Many founders jump in without a structured program. They figure things out by making mistakes. You have the chance to skip the most expensive mistakes entirely. But knowing alone does not cut risk. Action cuts risk. Use your self-assessment scores. Contact the professionals. Reach out to the organizations. Central Florida, and this campus in particular, has built an ecosystem specifically to help founders like you succeed. Use it.

questions.stem: A founder rates herself a '2' on the Legal Setup section of the readiness self-assessment. According to the scoring guide, what should she do before taking action on entity formation?
options: Proceed with forming an LLC since it is the most common choice for international founders
Re-read Module 1 and then file on [SunBiz](https://sunbiz.org) immediately
Consult a business attorney or CPA before filing anything, because a score of 1 or 2 signals the need for professional guidance
Skip entity formation and operate as a sole proprietor until she feels more confident
questions.explanation: The readiness self-assessment scoring guide states that any area rated 1 or 2 is a priority for professional consultation before taking action. Getting entity selection wrong costs more than the filing fee. It can mean months of restructuring and potential tax complications.

questions.stem: The program identifies four recurring themes that should guide every business decision. A founder is about to hire a private immigration consultant for $5,000. Which theme should she apply FIRST?
options: Understand the Culture — learn the cultural norms around immigration consulting
Watch Your Money — check whether the same service is available for free through SBDC or SCORE before paying a private consultant
Reduce Risk — hire the consultant immediately to avoid any immigration delays
Avoid Common Mistakes — ask other founders which consultant they used
questions.explanation: The 'Watch Your Money' theme reminds founders to check free resources before spending on private services. While immigration law specifically requires a licensed attorney (not SBDC), the broader principle applies: always verify whether government-funded programs or free advisory services cover your need before paying for private consulting.

questions.stem: A founder from Chile rated herself 4 out of 5 on market research and product readiness, but 1 out of 5 on legal setup and 2 out of 5 on U.S. taxes. She wants to start selling to U.S. customers next month. Based on the readiness framework in this lesson, how should she evaluate whether her timeline is realistic?
options: Her strong market readiness scores mean she is ready to start selling immediately regardless of other gaps
She should delay all U.S. activity for at least a year until every category reaches a 4 or 5
She should ignore the legal and tax scores since those can be fixed later without consequences

Her legal and tax scores of 1 and 2 signal that selling next month without professional guidance in those areas risks penalties, compliance issues, or structural problems that could cost far more than the delay — she should consult an attorney and CPA before taking on U.S. customers
questions.explanation: The readiness framework treats scores of 1 or 2 as priorities for professional consultation before taking action. Selling to U.S. customers without a properly formed entity, an understanding of tax obligations, or the right contracts creates liability exposure. Her strong market scores are valuable, but the legal and tax gaps must be addressed first. The solution is not to wait a year — it is to schedule consultations with a business attorney and CPA immediately, which could resolve the gaps in weeks rather than months.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.content: You do not need to have everything figured out. You need to know what to figure out first and who can help. That is what this program gave you. Your readiness self-assessment is not a test score. It is your pre-departure checklist. The areas where you rated yourself lowest are your highest-priority next steps. Apply your scores to prioritize next steps: evaluate which gaps need professional guidance first, then create a specific action plan for each one. You have arrived at campus headquarters for a reason. Use these priorities to guide your 1:1 conversation with the [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) team.

lessons.title: Resource Downloads & Digital Credential
objectives: Download the U.S. Market Entry Checklist, Florida Setup Roadmap, Central Florida Resource Guide, and Expansion Decision Worksheet
Understand the POK Tech digital credential and how to display it on LinkedIn

contentBlocks.title: Your Takeaway Resources from Campus HQ
contentBlocks.content:
With your readiness assessment complete, think of this as walking through the [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) resource center before heading out. You would not leave campus without picking up the materials that turn everything you learned into a real plan. These four downloadable resources do exactly that:

  1. U.S. Market Entry Checklist . Module-by-module action list from legal setup to ecosystem resources.
  2. Florida Setup Roadmap . Step-by-step sequence from entity formation to fully operational.
  3. Central Florida Resource Guide . Directory of organizations, contacts, and programs for international founders.
  4. Expansion Decision Worksheet . Structured go/no-go framework to consider whether now is the right time.

Download all four now. They pair directly with your 1:1 meeting with [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu).

contentBlocks.title: Did You Know?
contentBlocks.content: The UCF Business Incubation Program operates multiple sites across the greater Orlando region, providing over 100,000 square feet of incubation space. Since its founding, companies in the program have created thousands of jobs in Central Florida. UCF itself is one of the top patent-producing universities in the country, and the research partnerships between campus labs and incubator companies give founders access to talent and technology that would cost a fortune to build independently.

contentBlocks.title: Resource 1: U.S. Market Entry Checklist
contentBlocks.content:
This is your pre-departure checklist — every item, verified. The U.S. Market Entry Checklist turns every major topic from this program into action items grouped by module: legal setup, finances, IP protection, team building, market entry, culture, and ecosystem resources. Each item includes a priority level and suggested timeframe.

What does that mean for you? Tip: Start with the ecosystem section — it connects you with free resources before you start paying for services.

contentBlocks.title: Resource 2: Florida Setup Roadmap
contentBlocks.content:
This is your step-by-step sequence — what comes first, second, third. The Florida Setup Roadmap is sorted by order instead of topic, answering exactly that question. It walks through four phases: (1) pre-arrival preparation (remote), (2) entity formation and registration, (3) financial infrastructure, and (4) operational readiness. Each phase includes estimated timeframes and flags which steps require physical presence versus remote completion.

Many founders complete phases 1 and 2 remotely before arriving, so they can open a bank account in one visit with all documents ready.

contentBlocks.title: Resource 3: Central Florida Resource Guide
contentBlocks.content:
This is your campus directory — every support organization, one call away. The Central Florida Resource Guide puts all the groups from Module 7 into a single, easy-to-search reference. It covers business advising (SBDC, SCORE, [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu)), incubators and accelerators, government programs (SBA, Enterprise Florida), professional services (attorneys, CPAs, banking contacts), networking and community organizations, and details on what comes after this course through [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu).

Each entry includes the organization name, what they offer, and their website. Bookmark it and update it as you discover new resources through networking.

contentBlocks.title: Resource 4: Expansion Decision Worksheet
contentBlocks.content:
This is your go/no-go decision — the honest assessment before you commit. The Expansion Decision Worksheet helps you decide whether to enter the U.S. market now. It scores you across five areas: market fit, financial readiness, legal readiness, operations, and timing.

This is not pass/fail. A founder from Colombia scored 2 out of 5 on financial readiness, spent four months strengthening home-market revenue, re-scored at 4, and entered the U.S. with a much stronger foundation. The worksheet tells you what gap to close first.

Complete this before your 1:1 meeting with [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) — it helps the team give you targeted advice based on your specific readiness profile.

sections.heading: Legal & Formation
sections.content:
- **[SunBiz](https://sunbiz.org)** (sunbiz.org) — Florida business registration
- **[IRS EIN](https://www.irs.gov/businesses/small-businesses-self-employed/employer-id-numbers)** (irs.gov) — Free employer identification number
- **[LegalZoom](https://www.legalzoom.com)/[Incfile](https://www.incfile.com)** — Paid formation services if you want assistance
- **[Florida Bar Lawyer Referral](https://www.floridabar.org/public/lrs/)** — Find a vetted business attorney

sections.heading: Banking & Finance
sections.content:
- **[Mercury](https://mercury.com)** — Online banking for startups (international-friendly)
- **[Relay](https://relayfi.com)** — Free business banking with accounting integration
- **[Stripe Atlas](https://stripe.com/atlas)** — Formation + banking bundle for international founders
- **[QuickBooks Online](https://quickbooks.intuit.com)** — Most popular small business accounting software

sections.heading: Visas & Immigration
sections.content:
- **[USCIS](https://www.uscis.gov)** (uscis.gov) — Official immigration information
- **[Boundless](https://www.boundless.com)** — Immigration application assistance
- **[AILA Lawyer Search](https://www.ailalawyer.com)** — Find a qualified immigration attorney
- **[Fragomen](https://www.fragomen.com)** — International immigration law firm

sections.heading: Local Support
sections.content:
- **Florida [SBDC at UCF](https://sbdcorlando.com/)** — Free business consulting
- **[SCORE Orlando](https://orlando.score.org)** — Free mentorship from business veterans
- **[Orlando Economic Partnership](https://orlando.org)** — Relocation and expansion support
- **[Prospera](https://prosperausa.org/)** — Bilingual support for Hispanic entrepreneurs
- **[UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu)** — Business incubation programs

contentBlocks.title: Your Digital Credential
contentBlocks.content:
Your completion credential — proof you finished the program. When you complete this program, you earn a digital credential : a verified badge for your LinkedIn profile, website, or email signature. It validates completion of the [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) U.S. Market Readiness Program, backed by one of the largest and most active university incubation ecosystems in the country.

Now, here is where it gets tricky. The credential is issued through POK Tech, the same provider used by Arizona State University and other major universities. It is a program completion credential, not a professional certification — but it signals you took a structured, university-backed program seriously.

How to Claim Your Credential

After completing the program, you receive an email from POK Tech. The process takes five minutes:

  1. Open the email and select the claim link
  2. Set up your POK Tech account (or log in)
  3. Accept the credential
  4. Share to LinkedIn using the built-in sharing tool

The credential does not expire.

contentBlocks.title: Entrepreneur organizing resources and preparation materials for U.S. market entry

questions.stem: A founder has validated her market opportunity and wants to know the correct ORDER of steps to become operational in Florida — from entity formation through financial setup to hiring. Which downloadable resource is specifically designed for this purpose?
options: U.S. Market Entry Checklist — it covers every topic from the program in checklist format
Florida Setup Roadmap — it organizes steps by sequence (what to do first, second, third) rather than by topic
Expansion Decision Worksheet — it helps assess whether to enter the U.S. market at all
Central Florida Resource Guide — it lists organizations and contacts available to founders
questions.explanation: The Florida Setup Roadmap is organized by sequence rather than by topic. It walks founders through four phases : pre-arrival preparation, entity formation, financial infrastructure, and operational readiness — in the correct order. The Checklist organizes by module topic, while the Roadmap answers 'what do I do first, second, third?'

questions.stem: A founder completed the Expansion Decision Worksheet and scored high on market fit but low on financial readiness. Based on the lesson guidance, what is the most strategic response?
options: Enter the U.S. market immediately since market fit is the most important factor
Abandon U.S. expansion plans entirely since financial readiness is insufficient
Strengthen home-market revenue first to improve financial readiness, then revisit the worksheet before entering the U.S.
Apply for an SBA loan to cover the financial gap before entering the market
questions.explanation: The Expansion Decision Worksheet is not a pass/fail test. A founder from Colombia used it, found her financial score was 2 out of 5, spent four months strengthening home-market revenue, and re-scored at 4 before entering the U.S. with a stronger foundation. The worksheet tells you what gap to close first, not whether to give up.

questions.stem: A founder from Brazil has completed the [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) Phase 1 program and wants to use her digital credential to strengthen her professional profile when meeting potential U.S. partners. How should she apply the POK Tech credential?
options: Claim the credential through the POK Tech email link, add it to her LinkedIn profile using the built-in sharing tool, and reference it on her website — since the credential does not expire, it serves as an ongoing signal that she completed a structured, university-backed market readiness program
Print the credential and bring a physical copy to every business meeting as proof of her qualifications
Wait until she completes Phase 2 before claiming the credential, since the Phase 1 credential alone carries little weight
Use the credential to apply for SBA loans, since it serves as a professional certification recognized by federal agencies
questions.explanation: The POK Tech digital credential is designed to be shared digitally — on LinkedIn, websites, and email signatures. It validates completion of the [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) U.S. Market Readiness Program and does not expire. It is a program completion credential, not a professional certification, so it does not qualify for SBA lending or serve as a formal qualification. There is no reason to wait for Phase 2 — the Phase 1 credential stands on its own as a signal of structured preparation.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.content: The takeaway is this: do not leave headquarters without your equipment. These four resources are yours to keep, and they extend the value of this program beyond the screen and into your actual business planning. The checklist keeps you organized. The roadmap keeps you in sequence. The resource guide connects you to help. The decision worksheet keeps you honest about readiness. Apply these tools to your planning: download all four now, and bring them to your 1:1 meeting with the [UCF BIP](https://incubator.ucf.edu) team at campus headquarters. And claim your digital credential on LinkedIn. It is a small step, but it signals to everyone in your network that you are serious about doing this the right way.

lessons.title: Next Steps — Your UCF BIP Pathway
objectives: Schedule a 1:1 consultation with the UCF BIP team
Understand what your continued journey with UCF BIP offers beyond this program
Identify their top 3 priority actions for the next 30 days

contentBlocks.title: Your Next Move from Campus Headquarters
contentBlocks.content:
With your resources downloaded and your readiness scores in hand, you are standing at the front door of UCF's incubator complex. This is where the program stops being a course and starts being a relationship. One action matters most: schedule your 1:1 meeting with Brian Bedrick and the UCF BIP team.

What does this actually mean day to day? This is your orientation inside headquarters — the team gets to know your mission and helps you plan the route. It is a strategy session, not a sales call. A 30-minute conversation can save you months of trial and error.

What to Bring

Bring your readiness scores and your questions — that is your entry briefing for the team.

  • Your readiness self-assessment scores from Lesson 8-1
  • Your completed Expansion Decision Worksheet from Lesson 8-2
  • Your top 3 questions: written down before the meeting
  • Your business context : company, product, home market, and why you're considering the U.S.

Book Your Meeting Now

BOOK YOUR 1:1 MEETING

Free, low-pressure, and the natural next step.

contentBlocks.title: Founder at UCF 1:1 Meeting
contentBlocks.alt: Illustration of a founder shaking hands with a UCF business advisor across a desk in a warmly lit campus office, with a printed action plan, laptop, and coffee cups on the table.

contentBlocks.title: Did You Know?
contentBlocks.content: UCF is consistently ranked among the top universities in the nation for producing graduates ready to enter the workforce, and its proximity to major employers like Lockheed Martin, Siemens, and the space industry at nearby Cape Canaveral creates a unique talent pipeline. The Central Florida Research Park, adjacent to campus, is one of the largest research parks in the country, giving incubator companies access to specialized talent and research partnerships that would be difficult to find elsewhere.

contentBlocks.title: What Comes After Phase 1
contentBlocks.content:
Phase 1 gave you the map. Your continued journey with UCF BIP puts you on the road. This program is Phase 1: the foundation before spending money in the U.S. market. Some founders move forward independently. Others benefit from the next stages of the UCF BIP market entry/soft landing program:

  • Personalized advisory . One-on-one guidance specific to your business
  • Professional connections . Warm introductions to attorneys, CPAs, and banking contacts
  • Ecosystem access . Investors, industry contacts, and partner organizations
  • Peer community . Other international founders at a similar stage
  • Structured milestones . Progress tracking with regular check-ins

Details are discussed during your 1:1 meeting. Your conversation will help determine which path makes sense.

contentBlocks.title: Your Top 3 Actions for the Next 30 Days
contentBlocks.content:
These are your first three moves after leaving campus. Pick your top three actions for the next 30 days. Three, not ten. Specific actions lead to higher follow-through than long lists.

Make Each Action Specific

Here's how this plays out. Weak: "Learn more about entity types." Strong: "Schedule a consultation with a business attorney to discuss LLC vs. C-Corp for my visa situation by [date]."

High-impact 30-day actions:

  1. Book your 1:1 with UCF BIP . Do it today.
  2. Contact a specific professional . Attorney, CPA, or immigration lawyer. "Email this referral by Friday."
  3. Complete one research task . SunBiz name check, USPTO search, or compile bank documentation.
  4. Register for a free resource . SBDC counseling, SCORE mentorship, or a chamber event.
  5. Complete the Expansion Decision Worksheet . Block 60 minutes before your 1:1.

contentBlocks.title: Important Reminder Before You Go
contentBlocks.content:
This program provides general educational information to help international founders understand the U.S. business environment. It does not constitute legal, tax, immigration, or financial advice. Founders should consult qualified professionals before making business decisions.

This is not a formality. It is genuinely important. The information in this program gives you a strong foundation, but every founder's case is different. Your entity choice, your tax duties, your visa pathway, your IP strategy: all of these depend on your specific case. The experts you work with will take the general knowledge you gained here and tailor it to your particular business, your country of origin, your industry, and your goals. That is how knowledge becomes safe, real action.

contentBlocks.title: One Last Thing — Your Feedback Matters
contentBlocks.content:
Now, before you close this program, two questions. Your answers help make this better for every founder after you.

Was This Program Worth Your Time?

Did it change your understanding of U.S. market entry? Help you avoid at least one costly mistake? Be honest. Positive and critical answers help equally.

What Would Make It Better?

A topic in more depth? An area too basic? A resource you wished existed? Share your thoughts during your 1:1 or send them to the UCF BIP team.

tabs.label: Skills Gained
tabs.content:
**By completing this course, you've developed practical knowledge in:**

- U.S. business entity selection and registration
- Federal, state, and local tax compliance
- Intellectual property protection strategies
- Hiring and visa pathway planning
- U.S. market entry and customer acquisition
- Cross-cultural business communication
- Central Florida ecosystem navigation

tabs.label: Your Credential
tabs.content:
**POK Tech Digital Credential**

Your completion of this program earns you a verified digital credential from POK Tech.

**What this means:**
- Shareable on LinkedIn and professional profiles
- Verifiable by employers and partners
- Demonstrates commitment to professional development
- Recognized by UCF BIP program for Phase 2 eligibility

tabs.label: What's Next
tabs.content:
**This is the beginning, not the end.**

1. **Apply for UCF BIP Phase 2** — hands-on mentorship and market validation
2. **Book your SBDC consultation** — get personalized business planning support
3. **Connect with fellow founders** — join the UCF international entrepreneur network
4. **Take action this week** — choose ONE thing from this course and do it today

**Your U.S. business journey starts now.**

questions.stem: A founder has completed Phase 1 (this program), landed her first two U.S. customers, and generated early revenue. Which UCF BIP phase is designed for her current business stage?
options: Phase 1. She should retake the program to reinforce the fundamentals
Phase 2, designed for founders establishing initial U.S. operations with a validated opportunity
Phase 3, designed for founders with early traction (customers and revenue) who need help scaling
She has outgrown the UCF BIP program and should work independently
questions.explanation: Phase 3 of the UCF BIP market entry/soft landing program is specifically designed for founders who already have initial traction : early customers, revenue, or signed partnerships. It focuses on growth strategy, investor connections, expanded networking, and operational support for scaling. Phase 2 is for founders still establishing initial operations.

questions.stem: According to the lesson's guidance on effective goal-setting, which of the following is the STRONGEST action item for a founder's 30-day plan?
options: Schedule a 30-minute consultation with a CPA who works with foreign-owned businesses by March 28
Learn more about U.S. taxes
Research the market and figure out pricing
Get everything set up as soon as possible
questions.explanation: Effective action items must be specific, time-bound, and completable. 'Schedule a 30-minute consultation with a CPA who works with foreign-owned businesses by March 28' includes a specific action (schedule consultation), a specific qualifier (CPA with international experience), and a deadline. Vague goals like 'learn more' or 'research the market' lack the specificity needed for follow-through.

questions.stem: A founder from Guatemala has completed this Phase 1 program and wants to take action this week. She has not yet formed a U.S. entity, has no SBDC appointment, and has not completed the Expansion Decision Worksheet. Based on the lesson's guidance on prioritizing next steps, which specific set of actions should she take in the next seven days?
options: Immediately file an LLC on SunBiz and open a bank account so she can start accepting U.S. payments
Focus entirely on attending networking events to build connections before doing any administrative work
Wait until she feels fully confident in all seven module topics before taking any action
Book her 1:1 meeting with UCF BIP, schedule a free SBDC counseling session at floridasbdc.org, and block 60 minutes to complete the Expansion Decision Worksheet before the meeting
questions.explanation: The lesson emphasizes three specific first-week actions: book the UCF BIP 1:1 meeting (free, 30 minutes, the single most important next step), connect with a support resource like SBDC, and complete the Expansion Decision Worksheet so the BIP team can give targeted advice. Filing an LLC before consulting a professional risks choosing the wrong entity type. Waiting for full confidence delays progress unnecessarily. Networking matters, but structured support should come first.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.content: The door is open. The next move is yours. You have arrived at campus headquarters not as a finish line, but as a starting point. You have the knowledge, the resources, and the access to a team that wants to help you succeed. The founders who tend to get the most value from this program are the ones who take action within the first week. Apply what you learned: book your 1:1 meeting, then create your top 3 actions with specific deadlines. Take the first step today. Central Florida is ready for you, and the UCF BIP team is ready to help you get there.

moduleLearningOutcomes: Assess their personal readiness for U.S. market entry based on program content
Produce actionable deliverables including checklists, a Florida setup roadmap, and a U.S. market readiness assessment
Identify their logical next step (resources, Phase 2, or 1:1 consultation)
